reasoning: The latest FRED release on March 26 was 5.75%, an increase from 5.54% the prior week, driven by the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict that spiked 10-year Treasury yields to 4.26%. Daily rate tracking from Bankrate and Fortune shows that at the end of the period (March 26-27), daily 15-year fixed rates were generally quoted above the weekly average of 5.75% (e.g., Bankrate at 5.82-5.85% and Fortune at 5.73-5.77%). Because Freddie Mac averages the rates from the preceding days, the daily rates closing out the week were higher than the midpoint of the previous week. This mathematical tailwind means that if daily rates simply stay flat or only slightly decline in the days leading up to the April 2 release, the newly computed average will very likely exceed 5.75%. Thus, there is a strong probability (0.65) that the April 5 value (which reflects the April 2 release) will be higher than the March 29 value (the March 26 release of 5.75%).
